theme: The floor between syncs — give every idle moment breath, shadow, and soul
visual_observations: Desktop: the lower half of the floor is nearly empty — a wandering idle worker appears as a bare pulsing ring that reads as a loading spinner, not a person. The ficus plant growth stages are lovely but static. The window dark rectangle is easy to miss. No visual connection exists between co-workers despite them sharing tasks. Door arrivals animate the door widget but the floor itself is silent. Mobile: the wandering circle dominates the cramped lower floor and looks especially mechanical. Bottom half dead space is proportionally worse on small screens.

ideas.title: Idle Worker Thought Clouds
ideas.description: The wandering idle characters are currently bare pulsing rings with orbit dots — they look like loading spinners, not people on a break. Add a CSS cloud-shaped thought bubble (three stacked border-radius circles forming a classic thought cloud, distinct from the rectangular speech bubble style) that floats above wandering workers. Each worker has a curated IDLE_THOUGHTS entry in constants.js — Maya: ['🍕', '🎨', '😴', '🧋'], Carlos: ['☕', '🔧', '🤔', '🍜'], Dave: ['🚀', '💭', '🫠', '🎮'], Sofia: ['🔍', '🎵', '🧸', '🌙']. A thought picks randomly on each wander cycle start, floats up 14px, stays 3s, then fades over 1s before the next cycle. The cloud itself is 0.6rem, 40% opacity, pointer-events: none. The transition uses CSS @keyframes thought-float { 0% { opacity:0; transform: translateY(0) scale(0.6); } 20% { opacity: 0.9; transform: translateY(-6px) scale(1); } 80% { opacity: 0.7; transform: translateY(-14px) scale(1); } 100% { opacity: 0; transform: translateY(-18px) scale(0.8); } }. WHY: This is the single most impactful small change for the floor view. A drifting 🍕 above a wandering worker instantly reads as 'bored human daydreaming' rather than 'system process polling'. It costs 20 lines of CSS and 15 lines of JSX but transforms idle workers from mechanical orbiters into colleagues you recognise.

ideas.title: Collaboration Arcs Between Shared-Tag Workers
ideas.description: Workers share task types (Design, Bug, Ship, QA etc.) but the floor shows zero spatial relationship between them — there is no way to see who is working on the same problem. In Office.jsx, after computing workingWorkers, derive pairs: for each unique task tag (from getTaskTags()), collect workers sharing it, then enumerate pairs. Render these pairs as an SVG layer (position: absolute, inset: 0, pointer-events: none, z-index: 0) that sits beneath worker characters. Each pair gets a quadratic bezier path: M [desk1.cx, desk1.cy] Q [midX, midY - 12%floorHeight] [desk2.cx, desk2.cy]. Stroke is the tag's category color (Design = #a78bfa, Bug = #ef4444, Ship = #f97316, QA = #3b82f6) at 25% opacity, 1.2px width, stroke-dasharray: '6 4', animated with stroke-dashoffset decreasing from 40 to 0 over 2s linear infinite — giving the appearance of particles flowing between desks. Arc fades out when either worker's tag changes. WHY: The floor is a presence map but not a collaboration map. A flowing arc between 'Maya doing Design' and 'Carlos doing Design' communicates 'they're in the same problem space' with zero new data fields, zero API changes. It turns the floor into a live team topology.

ideas.title: Door Arrival Light Splash
ideas.description: doorEvent='arrive' already animates the door widget at the bottom of the floor. But the floor itself stays completely silent — nothing environmental responds. When doorEvent === 'arrive' fires, render a short-lived light element inside .office-floor at the entrance position (bottom-center, matching the .office-door location): a div with clip-path: polygon(30% 100%, 70% 100%, 90% 0%, 10% 0%) — a trapezoid representing light spilling inward through the door. background: radial-gradient(ellipse at 50% 100%, rgba(255,250,230,0.4) 0%, transparent 70%). It flashes in 0.12s (opacity 0→0.5) then fades out 1.1s (opacity 0.5→0). A second radial blur disc (24px blur, rgba(255,250,200,0.12)) sits at the door center for an additional 0.6s. Component mounts on doorEvent='arrive', self-removes after 1.3s via useEffect timeout. WHY: Light through a door when someone arrives is the most elemental piece of environmental storytelling possible. The door already animates — the floor should react. This single addition makes arrivals feel physical and cinematic, not administrative.

ideas.title: Vacant Desk Personality Props
ideas.description: Empty desks — no worker assigned from workingWorkers — are visual dead zones. The desk renders a dark monitor and nothing else. Add a DESK_PROPS map to constants.js: { 0: { emoji: '🪴', label: 'desk plant' }, 1: { emoji: '📚', label: 'stack of books' }, 2: { emoji: '🎧', label: 'headphones' }, 3: { emoji: '🫖', label: 'teapot' }, 4: { emoji: '🗒️', label: 'notepad' }, 5: { emoji: '🎲', label: 'desk toy' } }. In the desk render loop in Office.jsx, when a desk ID is not in the workingWorkers map, render the prop as a 0.6rem span centered inside the desk's monitor area (aria-label from label field, pointer-events: none). When a worker arrives at the desk, the prop fades out over 0.3s via a CSS transition on opacity. When they leave, it fades back in over 0.5s. WHY: A vacant desk is not an empty desk — it is somebody's desk when they're not there. Real-world empty desks have personality: a mug, a cactus, a sticky note. Props make absence feel like temporary departure rather than nonexistence. On the screenshots, 2-3 vacant desks are always visible and they read as dead grey rectangles. This costs ~15 lines total.

ideas.title: After-Hours Cleaner Bot
ideas.description: The after-hours vibe currently changes the grid line color to indigo and dims the manager monitor — that is the full extent of the night-shift narrative. Add a CSSonly cleaner bot that appears only when teamVibe.key === 'after-hours'. It is a single div (12×14px) styled as a small robot shape: a square body (#4b5563), two pixel-wheels (4×3px, bottom), an antenna (2×5px, top-center). No emoji — it should look like a tiny physical bot. It traverses the floor on a deterministic patrol path using a CSS keyframes animation: left-to-right across the bottom desk row (0%–30%, y=55%), diagonal to opposite corner (30%–45%), right-to-left across the top row (45%–70%, y=28%), back down center aisle (70%–100%). Total cycle: 40s linear infinite. It fades in (0.6s) when vibe enters after-hours, fades out (0.6s) when vibe changes. Suppress in prefers-reduced-motion. WHY: Night shift is a distinct office state but right now it's just a colour swap. A tiny autonomous bot slowly sweeping the empty floor is the most efficient possible environmental storytelling for after-hours. It makes the space feel inhabited-but-empty in exactly the right way.

ideas.title: Rising Code Symbol Particles on Working Desks
ideas.description: Working desks already have a typed-glyph bob animation and monitor glow. Add one more layer: an ephemeral code-symbol particle that rises from just above the desk monitor every 5-9 seconds (randomized per desk via CSS custom property --desk-seed). The particle is a single span inside .desk__monitor containing one character drawn randomly from the set ['{ }', '//', '→', '✓', '⌘', '&&', '▶']. It starts at opacity: 0, translateY: 0, then animates to opacity: 0.6 at 30%, then fades and rises to opacity: 0 at translateY: -16px over 0.9s ease-out. Use a CSS @keyframes code-rise, applied to the span via animation. Only render the span when desk status is working; remove it (or set animation: none) when idle. One span per desk, reused via CSS animation-iteration-count: infinite with varying animation-delay. WHY: The desk bob shows a person typing — but the desk itself never 'exhales' work. A single rising symbol every several seconds is subliminal: you don't consciously register it but the floor feels productive. It is the visual equivalent of keyboard sounds drifting through an open-plan office.

ideas.title: Floor Productivity Heatmap Toggle
ideas.description: The floor shows WHO is there but not HOW DENSE the work is spatially. Add a small 🌡️ button (20px, bottom-left of .office-floor, z-index 10, semi-transparent background with a 2px border matching --accent) that, when clicked, overlays a 7-second auto-dismissing heatmap layer. Implementation: a .floor-heatmap div (position: absolute, inset:0, pointer-events: none, z-index: 2) containing one .heatmap-node per active worker, positioned at that worker's desk coordinates. Each node is a 60×40px div with border-radius: 50%, filter: blur(18px), mix-blend-mode: screen, opacity: 0.55. Color by progress bucket: 0-30% = #ef4444 (red), 31-70% = #f59e0b (amber), 71-99% = #22c55e (green), idle/absent = hidden. On open, nodes scale from 0→1 over 0.4s staggered. A countdown border on the toggle button (CSS conic-gradient border depleting from 100%→0% over 7s) shows time remaining. Auto-close after 7s. A legend row (3 dots + labels: Blocked / In Progress / Crushing) appears at the bottom of the floor during active view. WHY: This collapses the entire team's progress state into one spatial scan — you see whether energy is distributed or stuck. It is the single biggest information-density upgrade the floor could get in one interaction. The 7-second auto-dismiss prevents it from becoming a permanent clutter layer.

hire_proposals.name: Noa
hire_proposals.justification: Three consecutive reviews have now independently arrived at the same gap: Bloberto's Office is visually rich but aurally and kinetcially inert. Maya, Carlos, Dave and Sofia are strong engineers who implement motion well, but nobody owns the ambient layer as a system — which is why animations keep appearing as isolated features rather than a coherent motion language. Noa's mandate: (1) design and implement a unified timing/easing token set that all floor animations share, (2) build the opt-in Web Audio API soundscape (procedural keyboard hum, coffee machine, distant typing — no external audio files, default muted, WCAG 2.1 compliant with a prominent mute button), and (3) own seasonal and time-aware office variations so the floor feels different in December vs July. This is not a junior CSS role — it requires a rare combination of motion design taste, Web Audio API fluency, and systems thinking. The hire unblocks at least four backlogged ideas across reviews 31 and 32.
